planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or set out divisions around the last spring frost.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and press it into the soil, since light aids germination, keeping it barely moist until sprouts appear. Give plants lean, well-drained soil and full sun, and avoid rich feeding that causes floppy stems. Shear off the flat white flower heads as they fade to encourage a second flush through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or plant out divisions after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and keep lightly moist, or set out young plants in spring in gritty, free-draining soil. Stake or plant in groups so the tall stems support one another, and cut back after bloom. Harvest the broad golden plates for drying just as the flowers open fully.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted stock in spring once soil is workable.

directions: Set plants at the same depth they grew in the pot, spacing them for good airflow to limit mildew. The very tall stems may need support in exposed sites or richer soil. Cut the huge flat gold heads for fresh or dried arrangements when the color deepens in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant in lean, sharply drained soil and water only until established, then let it fend for itself. Deadhead spent heads to keep the clump tidy and prolong the display. Cut stems for drying when the deep yellow plates are fully colored in summer.

planting.note: Set out potted plants or divisions in spring.

directions: Plant in full sun and average-to-poor soil, avoiding overly fertile beds that weaken the stems. This sterile hybrid needs no deadheading to prevent seeding but shears well for rebloom. Harvest the sturdy mustard-gold flat heads for long-lasting dried bunches.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring; a compact selection good for containers.

directions: Set this dwarf form in full sun with excellent drainage, ideal at the front of a border or in a pot. Its tidy habit needs little staking, and shearing keeps it blooming. Trim faded bright-yellow heads to encourage repeat flushes through summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for first-year bloom, or set out plants in spring.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep barely moist, as it needs light to sprout. Expect some color variation from seed, with most plants opening cherry-pink and fading paler with age. Deadhead regularly for a long show and to keep self-sowing in check.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or plant out in spring.

directions: Sow on the soil surface with light and warmth, and grow on in lean, gritty ground. Flowers open bright cherry-red with a small yellow eye and soften to salmon and cream as they age. Shear the clump after the first flush to spark repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; grown vegetatively for color stability.

directions: Set plants in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing for airflow to reduce mildew. Prized for holding its deep red color longer than most millefolium types before fading. Deadhead spent heads to keep the display fresh and encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted stock in spring.

directions: Give it full sun and lean soil for sturdy stems and the best color range. Blooms open a warm burnt-orange and shift through peach to buff-yellow, giving a two-tone effect on the clump. Shear after the first flush and divide every few years to renew vigor.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant in full sun and gritty soil, keeping feeding minimal for compact growth. Flowers emerge brick-red to orange with a yellow center and age to softer coppery tones. Cut back spent stems to prompt a fresh flush and divide clumps periodically.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or set out plants in spring.

directions: Surface-sow with light for germination and grow on in lean, well-drained soil. Soft mauve-pink flat heads deepen and fade over their long bloom, drawing butterflies. Deadhead often to lengthen the season and limit seeding.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for bloom the first summer.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist soil and leave uncovered, as light speeds sprouting. This seed mix yields a blend of soft pastels including cream, pink, salmon, lavender, and buff. Deadhead to prolong flowering and shear the clump in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Set plants in lean, sharply drained soil and full sun for compact, sturdy growth. Rosy-pink heads with pale centers fade gracefully over a long bloom period. Shear spent flowers to keep the plant tidy and encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted plants in spring.

directions: Grow in full sun and free-draining soil, avoiding rich feeding that flops the stems. Soft lilac-mauve heads fade to silvery lavender for an airy effect in the border. Deadhead through the season for continued flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or plant out in spring; can spread by runners.

directions: Unlike most yarrows this species tolerates moister soil and carries clusters of small white button flowers. Surface-sow seed and keep lightly moist to germinate. Watch its spreading roots in small beds and deadhead to prolong the bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; spreads readily and is best divided often.

directions: This selection bears double, pompon-like pure white flowers on wiry stems. Give it average, evenly moist soil and full sun to light shade. Divide every couple of years to control its vigorous spread and cut stems for fresh bouquets.

planting.note: Plant potted stock or divisions in spring.

directions: A silvery-foliaged seedling of 'Moonshine' with soft primrose-yellow heads that pale as they age. Grow in full sun and sharp drainage for the best gray leaves. Shear after flowering to keep the habit compact and encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or plant out in spring as a low groundcover.

directions: This low, mat-forming species has soft, wooly gray-green foliage and flat yellow heads. Surface-sow seed and grow in gritty, sharply drained soil, as it dislikes wet feet. Use it in rock gardens or as edging and shear off spent blooms.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a rock garden or dry edge.

directions: A compact woolly yarrow selection with bright golden-yellow flat heads over silvery mats. Plant in lean, fast-draining soil in full sun and avoid overwatering. Trim spent flowers and lightly shear to keep the cushion dense.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or plant out in spring in a rock garden.

directions: A silvery alpine yarrow with small daisy-like white flowers, unusual among yarrows. Give it very sharp drainage, full sun, and lean gritty soil in a rock garden or trough. Keep it dry in winter and remove faded blooms to tidy the plant.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or plant out in spring; a rock-garden species.

directions: Grown mainly for its silky silver-white lobed foliage topped by small white flower clusters. Plant in gritty, fast-draining soil in full sun and protect from winter wet. Deadhead spent stems and shear lightly to maintain the compact silver mound.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a rock garden or trough.

directions: A dwarf hybrid forming low gray-green mats topped with soft primrose-yellow buttons. Ideal for the front of a rockery in sharp, gritty drainage and full sun. Shear off faded heads to keep the tidy cushion blooming through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted plants in spring.

directions: Also sold as 'The Beacon', this cultivar opens vivid scarlet-red with a small yellow eye. Grow in full sun and lean soil for the sturdiest stems and richest color. Deadhead to prolong bloom, as the red fades toward pink with age.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Named 'Fireland' in English, it opens fiery red-orange and matures to golden-yellow, often two-toned at once. Plant in lean, well-drained soil in full sun for strong upright stems. Shear after the first flush to encourage a second round of bloom.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring; part of the Tutti Frutti series.

directions: A Tutti Frutti selection with large heads of intense magenta-red that hold color well. Grow in full sun and free-draining soil, keeping fertility low for stout stems. Deadhead spent heads to extend the long summer display.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring; a Tutti Frutti series cultivar.

directions: Opens rosy pink and matures to a softer coral-pink and cream blend on the same clump. Plant in full sun and gritty soil for the sturdiest stems. Shear the plant after peak bloom to prompt fresh flowering.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring; a Seduction series selection.

directions: Bears brick-red to strawberry heads with golden centers that soften with age. Grow in full sun and lean, well-drained soil, avoiding rich beds. Deadhead consistently to keep the compact clump flowering into late summer.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring; a Seduction series cultivar.

directions: Carries bright rose-pink heads with small pale centers on a sturdy, compact clump. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage for strong, upright stems. Shear spent flowers to lengthen the bloom season and refresh the foliage.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring; a Seduction series selection.

directions: Produces soft butter-yellow flat heads on a tidy, mounded clump. Grow in full sun and lean, free-draining soil for the best form. Deadhead through summer to prolong the display and keep plants neat.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring; a dwarf New Vintage series cultivar.

directions: A compact, well-branched selection with deep red heads good for the front of a border or containers. Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, and its short habit rarely needs staking. Deadhead to maintain a long, tidy bloom.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring; a compact New Vintage series cultivar.

directions: A short, densely branched yarrow with rich rose-pink flat heads. Plant in full sun and gritty soil, ideal for small spaces and pots. Shear off faded flowers to keep it blooming and compact.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring; a tall Firefly series cultivar.

directions: A vigorous, tall Firefly selection with warm peach-apricot heads on strong stems. Give it full sun and lean, well-drained soil to keep stems upright. Deadhead spent flowers to extend the long summer show.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring; a Firefly series cultivar.

directions: A tall, upright yarrow with rich purple-pink amethyst heads that hold their color well. Plant in full sun and free-draining soil for the sturdiest stems. Cut back spent heads to keep the tall clump flowering.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring; a white Firefly series cultivar.

directions: A tall selection with crisp white flat heads over dark green foliage. Grow in full sun and gritty, well-drained soil for upright stems. Deadhead to prolong the bloom and cut stems for arrangements.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: A muted German selection whose heads open dusky rose and fade to sandstone and cream. Plant in lean, well-drained soil in full sun for the best stem strength. Shear after the first flush to keep the clump blooming.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Opens soft apricot-yellow and matures to a creamy pale yellow, giving a gentle two-tone look. Grow in full sun and free-draining soil, keeping feeding light. Deadhead spent heads to lengthen the flowering season.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted stock in spring.

directions: A robust hybrid with large sulphur-yellow heads that age to soft cream over green ferny foliage. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, giving it room as it forms a big clump. Shear after bloom and divide every few years to renew vigor.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Opens rich golden-orange and softens to buff and gold as the heads mature. Grow in lean, sharply drained soil in full sun to keep stems firm. Deadhead to prolong bloom and cut stems for drying at peak color.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; sold in English as Salmon Beauty.

directions: Part of the Galaxy hybrids, it opens warm salmon-pink and fades to a soft peachy cream. Plant in full sun and free-draining soil for the sturdiest stems. Shear the clump after the first flush to encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted stock in spring.

directions: A Galaxy hybrid with clear rose-pink heads and pale centers that age gently. Grow in full sun and lean, well-drained soil, avoiding rich feeding. Deadhead through summer to keep the plant blooming and tidy.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: A compact, silvery-leaved hybrid carrying flat mustard-yellow heads over feathery gray foliage. Plant in sharp drainage and full sun for the best foliage color. Shear spent flowers to keep the low clump neat and reblooming.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for first-year bloom.

directions: A seed strain producing a wide mix of colors including red, pink, salmon, cream, and white. Surface-sow the fine seed with light and warmth to germinate. Grow in lean soil and full sun, and deadhead to prolong the varied display.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or plant out in spring.

directions: Bears deep wine-red to blackcurrant heads with small pale centers, holding color better than many reds. Press seed onto moist soil and leave uncovered for light. Grow in full sun and gritty soil, and deadhead to lengthen bloom.

planting.note: Set out dormant tuberous roots in early spring or plant divisions in autumn.

directions: Plant the tuberous crown about two inches deep in cool, humus-rich soil that stays evenly moist, ideally where afternoons are lightly shaded. Stake tall stems in windy sites and cut back after flowering to tidy the clump. Deep blue hooded flowers open along upright spires in mid to late summer. Every part is highly poisonous, so wear gloves when handling roots.

planting.note: Set out potted plants or divisions in spring after the soil warms.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile soil kept reliably moist, mulching in summer to keep roots cool. This selection holds itself upright on thick stems and seldom needs support. Rich amethyst-blue spikes appear in September and October, prized as one of the last perennials to bloom. Handle with gloves and keep away from children and pets.

planting.note: Plant divisions or containers in spring or early autumn.

directions: Choose a spot with deep, moisture-holding soil and dappled light for the tallest stems. Water generously through dry spells and stake only in exposed borders. Long tapering panicles of soft lavender-blue flowers open in early to mid autumn. Every part of the plant is dangerously poisonous.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring into cool, rich ground.

directions: Plant in humus-rich soil that never fully dries, giving each crown room to build a strong clump. Keep it evenly watered and cut spent stems to the base in late fall. Deep violet flowers open very late, often into October, extending the border season. Wear gloves when dividing the toxic tuberous roots.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring after frosts ease.

directions: Give this unusual pink form cool, moist, fertile soil and shade from midday heat, which helps hold flower color. Water steadily and avoid letting the crown dry out. Soft dusky-pink hoods appear on upright stems in early autumn. As with all monkshoods, treat the whole plant as poisonous.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring into moist, well-worked soil.

directions: This selection is grown as much for its dark bronze-purple foliage as its flowers, so site it where the leaves show against lighter plants. Keep the soil rich and evenly moist for best leaf color. Purple-tinted flowers rise above the dusky foliage in early fall. All parts are highly toxic.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or autumn into deep, moist soil.

directions: Set in cool, fertile ground that holds moisture and give light shade in hot regions. The branching stems may need discreet staking in rich soil. Distinctive blue-and-white bicolored hoods open through mid and late summer on well-branched spikes. Keep the poisonous plant away from where food is grown or handled.

planting.note: Plant in spring into rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Prized for its rigid, self-supporting spires, this compact hybrid rarely needs staking even in open borders. Grow in fertile, evenly moist soil and water during droughts. Dense spikes of deep violet-blue flowers stand tightly upright from mid to late summer. Every part is poisonous, so wash hands after handling.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring into cool, fertile ground.

directions: A shorter hybrid well suited to the front or middle of a moist border, it keeps its stems upright without support. Give it rich soil and steady moisture through summer. Compact spikes carry blue-and-white patterned hoods in midsummer. Handle the toxic roots and foliage with care.

planting.note: Plant in spring into deep, moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: Site in cool, fertile soil kept evenly damp and lightly shaded in warm climates. Support the flowering stems if the soil is very rich. White hoods edged and flushed with violet-blue open through mid to late summer. As with every monkshood, all parts are dangerously poisonous.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into rich, moist soil.

directions: This tall, loosely branched monkshood suits the back of a moist border and benefits from staking in wind. Grow in deep, fertile soil that stays cool and damp at the roots. Widely branched sprays of very dark indigo-blue flowers open from mid to late summer. Keep this poisonous plant well away from vegetables.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring into cool, evenly moist ground.

directions: Grown for its unusual metallic silvery-blue coloring, this hybrid shines in a lightly shaded, moist border. Provide fertile soil and consistent water, cutting stems back after bloom. Steely gray-blue hoods open on branching stems in early to midsummer. All parts remain toxic, so handle with gloves.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: This early, bushy monkshood forms a neat mound and blooms well before the blue kinds. Grow in cool, rich soil with reliable moisture and some shade in hot climates. Spikes of creamy ivory-white hoods open in late spring and early summer. Every part of the plant is poisonous.

planting.note: Sow cold-stratified seed or plant divisions in spring.

directions: Plant in cool woodland-type soil rich in leaf mold and kept lightly moist, where it tolerates shade better than most. Water in dry spells and allow the tall stems to lean naturally among shrubs. Narrow, hooded pale yellow flowers open on branching stems in summer. The whole plant is highly toxic.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall into humus-rich, cool soil.

directions: Give this slender woodland monkshood a cool, leafy soil in partial shade and steady moisture. Let the arching stems weave through neighboring perennials for informal support. Elongated, helmet-shaped straw-yellow flowers appear through summer. Treat all parts as dangerously poisonous.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the base of a shrub or trellis in cool soil.

directions: Unlike its upright relatives, this species twines its stems through shrubs and up supports, so plant it where it can scramble. Keep the roots cool and moist in shade while letting the top reach light. Hooded blue-purple flowers hang along the climbing stems in late summer and autumn. All parts are poisonous.

planting.note: Sow stratified seed or plant divisions in spring into gritty soil.

directions: This compact alpine species prefers sharply drained, gritty soil and tolerates more sun and drought than the tall border kinds. Avoid rich, wet ground, which causes it to flop. Short, dense spikes of pale yellow hoods open in mid to late summer above finely cut foliage. As with all monkshoods, the plant is toxic.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a cool, moist, shaded spot.

directions: A native of eastern North American woodlands and streambanks, it wants consistently moist, humus-rich soil and shade. Its weak stems often lean on surrounding plants, so grow it among sturdier perennials. Loose clusters of blue-violet hoods open in late summer and early fall. Every part is poisonous.

planting.note: Plant in spring into deep, moist soil in cool shade.

directions: This western North American native grows along mountain streams and wet meadows, needing rich soil that never dries out. Provide part shade and abundant moisture through the growing season. Tall stems carry deep blue-purple hoods from mid to late summer. Handle carefully, as all parts are highly toxic.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into cool, moist woodland soil.

directions: A rare Appalachian native, it produces reclining, sprawling stems in cool, damp, shaded woodland soil. Give it steady moisture and let the lax stems ramble over the ground or nearby plants. Creamy white hoods open on the trailing stems in summer. As with all monkshoods, every part is poisonous.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into fertile, moist soil.

directions: This European mountain species has loosely branched stems best grown in cool, moist, humus-rich soil with some shade. Water in dry weather and support tall stems in open borders. Blue or blue-and-white hoods, sometimes patterned, open on branching stems through summer. The whole plant is dangerously toxic.

planting.note: Plant in spring into rich, moisture-holding soil.

directions: A tall, loosely branched Chinese species suited to the back of a moist, part-shaded border. Keep the soil deep and cool and stake in windy positions. Branching stems carry deep blue-violet hoods from mid to late summer. Every part of the plant is highly poisonous.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into deep, moist, fertile soil.

directions: Grow this east Asian monkshood in cool, moisture-retentive soil with light shade in warmer areas. Water through drought and cut stems back after flowering. Sturdy stems bear large blue hoods in late summer into autumn. Handle the toxic roots and stems with gloves.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall into cool, rich soil.

directions: This white-flowered form of common monkshood lights up a shaded border and wants the same deep, evenly moist soil as the species. Water in dry spells and stake tall stems if needed. Pure white hoods rise on upright spikes in mid to late summer. All parts remain highly poisonous.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: This soft pink selection colors best in cool climates and partial shade, fading paler where summers run hot. Keep the soil deep, fertile, and reliably moist. Dusky pink hoods open on upright stems in mid to late summer. Every part of the plant is poisonous, so wear gloves when dividing.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or autumn into cool, fertile soil.

directions: This garden hybrid group thrives in deep, moisture-retentive soil with light afternoon shade. Water during dry weather and support the flowering stems in exposed sites. Branching spikes of blue, violet, or bicolored hoods open from mid to late summer. Keep this toxic plant away from food crops and pets.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into cool, moist, leafy soil.

directions: A robust species from northern woods and meadows, it forms a leafy clump in cool, humus-rich soil with some shade. Provide steady moisture and let it settle in undisturbed for years. Tall spikes of dull violet to purple hoods open in early to midsummer. The whole plant is dangerously poisonous.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed outdoors in autumn, or lift and split mature crowns in early spring.

directions: Cover seed lightly and keep it cool and damp; sprouting can stretch across two winters. This uncommon native prefers moist limestone-rich woodland soil and consistent shade. Slender white flower wands appear in late summer above broad maple-like leaves.

planting.note: Sow ripe seed in fall for cold stratification, or plant divisions in spring.

directions: Barely cover the seed and hold it moist through a winter chill before expecting growth. This mountain native wants rich, cool, moist soil in the shade of taller trees. Wispy white spikes rise in late summer, a touch airier than those of black cohosh.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed outdoors in autumn, or divide clumps in early spring.

directions: Sow onto moist ground and leave lightly covered for the winter cold to break dormancy. Give it deep, fertile, reliably moist soil in partial shade with room to reach its full height. Branching white plumes open in early autumn and carry a light sweet scent.

planting.note: Sow ripe seed in fall, or set out divisions in spring.

directions: Keep barely-covered seed cool and moist through winter to prompt sprouting. This compact species has bold maple-shaped leaves and appreciates rich, moist shade. Upright white spikes appear in mid to late autumn on a tidy clump.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed outdoors in autumn, or divide established plants in spring.

directions: Barely cover seed and let it overwinter cold and damp before growth begins. Plant in cool, moist, organic soil with afternoon shade and steady moisture. Slender white bottlebrush spikes open late in the season, among the last bugbanes to bloom.

planting.note: Sow ripe seed outdoors in fall, or divide crowns in spring.

directions: Sow shallowly and let the seed weather a cold winter to break dormancy. This compact woodlander wants moist, calcareous, shaded soil and steady moisture. Small white flower spikes in late spring are followed by clusters of shiny black berries, which are poisonous.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or early autumn into moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant in deep, moisture-retentive soil with some morning light to darken the near-black foliage. Keep it well watered through summer and shelter from drying wind. Tall arching spikes of fragrant white flowers open in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring or fall in rich, moist soil.

directions: Give it cool, fertile, evenly moist soil and a spot with gentle morning sun to hold the dark leaf color. Avoid letting the ground dry, which stresses the plant and dulls the foliage. Fragrant creamy-white bottlebrush wands appear in early autumn against the deep purple leaves.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring or early fall into moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant this more compact selection in fertile, reliably moist soil with a little morning light. Its dark bronze-purple foliage stays tidy and suits smaller shade borders. Fragrant white spikes rise in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted plants in spring or fall in cool, moist soil.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in rich, moist ground in partial shade. Bright green foliage forms a fresh mound below the flowers. Pearl-like green buds open to white spikes in autumn, followed by decorative seed heads.

planting.note: Plant container stock or divisions in spring or early autumn into moist, fertile soil.

directions: Grow in cool, moist, humus-rich soil with morning sun to deepen the chocolate-purple leaves. This more compact, sturdy selection needs little staking. Purple-pink buds open to fragrant mauve-tinged white spikes in early fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted plants in spring or fall in rich, moist soil.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile, evenly moist soil with a bit of morning light for the darkest foliage. Keep the roots cool and never allow drought. Purple-flushed buds open to fragrant pinkish spikes in early autumn above bronze-purple leaves.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall for a range of purple-leaved seedlings, or plant divisions in spring.

directions: Seed-raised plants in this group show varying degrees of purple leaf color, best expressed with morning sun. Grow in rich, moist, cool soil and keep watered through summer. Fragrant white to blush spikes open in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted stock in spring or early fall into moist, fertile soil.

directions: Give it deep, moist, rich soil with morning light to bring out the dark purple foliage and stems. Water consistently and shelter from strong wind. Tall fragrant white spikes emerge from purple-tinted buds in early autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or fall in deep, moist soil.

directions: Give this vigorous, tall selection deep, fertile, reliably moist soil and room to spread. Green foliage forms a large mound that may need shelter from wind at full height. Branching white flower spikes open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted stock in spring or fall in moist, shaded soil.

directions: Plant in rich, moist, well-drained woodland soil in partial to full shade. This selection is prized for its distinct blue-green foliage all season. White spring flowers ripen to white doll's-eye berries on red stalks; the berries are poisonous.

planting.note: Begin indoors and move out after frost into lean, well-drained ground.

directions: Barely cover the seed and provide warmth and sharp drainage for best results. Plant in full sun and lean soil; too much richness or water shortens its life. Tubular orange blooms appear from early summer to frost and are a magnet for hummingbirds.

planting.note: Start under lights and transplant out after frost into gritty soil.

directions: Sow the seed shallowly in a fast-draining mix and keep it just moist until it sprouts. Site it in full sun with excellent drainage, as soggy winter soil is its main enemy. Rose-pink tubular flowers scent the air and bloom from late summer well into fall.

planting.note: Frost-sensitive; start indoors and plant out only after nights stay warm.

directions: Surface-sow and keep warm, as this species germinates readily in heat. Grow in full sun with average water and good drainage; in cold zones treat it as an annual or overwinter cuttings. Rose to red spikes carry a lemony fragrance and bloom from summer until frost.

planting.note: Western native; cold stratification improves and evens out germination.

directions: Stratify the seed cold and moist for a few weeks, then sow into well-drained soil. This vigorous western native handles a range of conditions from full sun to light shade. Pale pink to white flower clusters bloom through summer and are heavily worked by bees and hummingbirds.

planting.note: Start indoors in spring and set out into gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow onto the surface of a fast-draining mix and keep barely moist until seedlings emerge. This compact southwestern native thrives on lean soil, full sun, and infrequent water. Soft pink to lavender flower spikes bloom through summer and draw bees and hummingbirds.

planting.note: A sterile hybrid grown from plants or division, not seed.

directions: Set container plants at the same depth they grew, spacing them for good airflow. Grow in full sun and average, well-drained soil; this hybrid is sterile so it blooms long without self-sowing. Powdery blue flower spikes cover the plant from midsummer to fall and are alive with bees.

planting.note: Grown from vegetative plants or division rather than seed.

directions: Plant into full sun and free-draining soil, spacing to let air move between clumps. Deadhead spent spikes to keep the show going and leave the last flush standing for winter interest. Smoky violet-blue flowers open from dark buds through summer and autumn, humming with pollinators.

planting.note: A patented selection propagated vegetatively, not from seed.

directions: Set plants in full sun and well-drained soil, watering to establish then easing off. The unusually thick, deep violet-blue flower spikes are large for the genus and hold color well. Bloom runs from midsummer into fall, drawing bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds.

planting.note: Comes true from seed; start indoors for best golden foliage color.

directions: Surface-sow and grow the seedlings in bright light to bring out the chartreuse-gold leaf color. Full sun keeps the foliage brightest, while too much shade turns it greener. Lavender-blue flower spikes rise above the golden leaves in summer and attract bees and butterflies.

planting.note: A named hybrid grown from plants or cuttings, not seed.

directions: Plant in full sun and sharply drained soil, giving this tall selection room to reach its full height. Water to establish, then let it run dry, as lean conditions keep it sturdy and long-lived. Deep raspberry-pink flowers with red-purple calyces bloom from summer to frost and pull in hummingbirds.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out after frost into gritty, fast-draining soil.

directions: Barely cover the seed and grow it warm and on the dry side to avoid rot. Give plants full sun and excellent drainage, and skip rich feeding that softens growth. Sunset-orange flowers touched with pink glow above narrow fragrant leaves from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Seed-grown selection; start indoors for bloom the first season.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it warm and lightly moist until it sprouts. This compact selection blooms quickly from seed and thrives on lean soil and full sun. Soft apricot-orange tubular flowers cover the plant from summer to frost and welcome hummingbirds.

planting.note: Compact seed strain; start indoors early for first-year flowers.

directions: Press the fine seed onto a warm mix and leave it uncovered, as light speeds sprouting. This dwarf selection suits containers and edges and blooms fast from seed in full sun. Bright orange flowers appear from early summer through fall and are irresistible to hummingbirds.

planting.note: Seed strain; start indoors and set out after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow and grow seedlings warm and bright for compact, floriferous plants. Plant into full sun and quick-draining soil, keeping water light once established. Golden-orange to apricot flowers bloom all summer into fall and draw hummingbirds and butterflies.

planting.note: A hybrid grown from plants or cuttings, not from seed.

directions: Set plants in full sun and gritty, well-drained soil, watering only to establish them. This hybrid resents wet feet, so avoid heavy clay and winter moisture. Coppery-orange to rose tubular flowers glow from summer to frost and are covered with hummingbirds.

planting.note: Grown from vegetative plants for its bronze-tinted foliage.

directions: Plant in full sun and free-draining soil to keep the dark bronze-green foliage richly colored. Water sparingly once rooted in, as this compact selection prefers lean, dry conditions. Rosy-pink flower spikes rise over the bronze leaves from summer to fall and lure pollinators.

planting.note: A named hybrid grown from plants rather than seed.

directions: Set plants in full sun and well-drained soil, giving them space for good airflow. Keep watering moderate and avoid rich feeding so stems stay upright and sturdy. Warm yellow to peach flower spikes bloom from summer into autumn and feed bees and hummingbirds.

planting.note: A patented selection propagated from cuttings, not seed.

directions: Plant in full sun and average, well-drained soil, spacing to keep air moving. This compact selection blooms heavily and needs little deadheading to keep going. Rose-pink flowers held in dark calyces cover the plant from early summer to frost, buzzing with bees.

planting.note: A dwarf hybrid grown from cuttings, not seed.

directions: Plant this compact selection in full sun and sharply drained soil for best winter survival. Its short, dense habit suits containers and front-of-border spots with little care. Soft golden-yellow flowers bloom from early summer through fall and attract hummingbirds and bees.

planting.note: A dwarf hybrid propagated vegetatively, not from seed.

directions: Set plants in full sun and well-drained soil, keeping water light once they root in. The naturally dwarf, tidy habit needs no staking and works well in pots. Coral-orange flowers bloom from early summer to frost and are a hummingbird favorite.

planting.note: A dwarf hybrid grown from cuttings, not seed.

directions: Plant in full sun and fast-draining soil, avoiding wet ground that shortens its life. Its compact form holds up without support and thrives on lean conditions. Bright mandarin-orange flowers bloom all summer into fall and draw in hummingbirds and butterflies.

planting.note: A dwarf hybrid propagated from cuttings, not seed.

directions: Grow in full sun and gritty, well-drained soil, watering just enough to establish. The short, bushy habit is well suited to containers and the front of a sunny border. Blended cream, pink, and peach flowers open from summer to frost and welcome pollinators.

planting.note: A dwarf hybrid grown vegetatively, not from seed.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, easing off water once the roots take hold. This compact, mildew-resistant selection needs no staking and suits small spaces. Clear yellow flowers bloom from early summer into fall and are worked by bees and hummingbirds.

planting.note: A dwarf hybrid propagated from cuttings, not seed.

directions: Set plants in full sun and sharply drained soil, keeping moisture low after establishment. The naturally short, dense form holds its shape without support in beds or pots. Reddish-orange flowers bloom from summer to frost and are a strong draw for hummingbirds.

planting.note: A dwarf hybrid grown from cuttings, not seed.

directions: Plant this very compact selection in full sun and quick-draining soil, ideal for pots and edges. Keep water light and skip rich feeding to preserve its tight, mounded habit. Bright orange flowers with dark calyces bloom from summer to frost and pull in hummingbirds.

planting.note: A dwarf hybrid propagated vegetatively, not from seed.

directions: Set plants in full sun and free-draining soil, watering only enough to get them going. The compact, uniform habit suits containers and the front of a sunny bed. Deep violet-blue flower spikes bloom from early summer into fall and are alive with bees.

planting.note: A compact hybrid grown from cuttings, often used as an annual in cold zones.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, treating it as a season-long bloomer in pots or beds. Water lightly and deadhead if you like, though it flowers freely on its own. Warm orange blooms appear from early summer to frost and are a reliable hummingbird magnet.

planting.note: A named hybrid grown from plants or cuttings, not seed.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing to allow good air circulation. Keep moisture moderate and cut back after the main flush to prompt fresh spikes. Deep cherry-red to burgundy flowers bloom from summer into autumn, feeding bees and butterflies.

planting.note: A named hybrid grown from plants rather than seed.

directions: Set plants in full sun and gritty, well-drained soil, watering to establish then backing off. The arching spikes hold their color for weeks and rarely need deadheading. Smoky violet-purple flowers bloom from midsummer to frost and draw bees and hummingbirds.

planting.note: A tall hybrid grown from plants or cuttings, not seed.

directions: Give this large hybrid full sun, well-drained soil, and room to reach its full stature. Water moderately and site it toward the back of a border where its height reads well. Lavender-blue flowers bloom from midsummer through fall and hum with bees and butterflies.

planting.note: A named hybrid grown from cuttings, not from seed.

directions: Plant in full sun and sharply drained soil, keeping water light once established. Avoid heavy, wet ground, which this pink-flowered selection dislikes in winter. Soft pink tubular flowers bloom from summer to frost and are a steady draw for hummingbirds.

planting.note: Frost-tender; start indoors and set out only after warm nights settle in.

directions: Surface-sow the seed in warmth and grow the seedlings bright until they can go out. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil; in cold climates grow it as an annual. Wine-red flower spikes with a citrus scent bloom from summer to frost and attract hummingbirds.

planting.note: A patented selection propagated from cuttings, not seed.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, watering to establish then easing off. Its bushy, upright habit blooms freely and needs no staking or heavy grooming. Soft peach flowers held in rosy calyces bloom from summer to frost and welcome hummingbirds.

planting.note: A variegated sport grown from cuttings to keep its leaf pattern true.

directions: Plant in full sun to light shade and average, well-drained soil, spacing for airflow. Full sun keeps the cream-and-green variegated foliage crisp and bright. Powdery blue flower spikes rise over the marbled leaves in summer and attract bees.

planting.note: A compact selection grown from cuttings, not seed.

directions: Plant this compact form in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing to keep air moving. Its dwarf habit suits smaller borders and containers with little upkeep. Smoky violet-blue flower spikes bloom from midsummer to fall and buzz with bees and butterflies.

planting.note: A named hybrid grown from cuttings, not from seed.

directions: Set plants in full sun and sharply drained soil, keeping water light once rooted. Lean, dry conditions keep the stems sturdy and improve winter survival. Bright raspberry-pink flowers bloom from summer to frost and are a favorite of hummingbirds.

planting.note: A selection of Texas hummingbird mint grown from cuttings, not seed.

directions: Plant in full sun and gritty, fast-draining soil, watering only to establish it. This compact selection stays neater than the species and dislikes wet winter soil. Lavender-rose tubular flowers bloom from summer into fall and pull in hummingbirds.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring once the soil has warmed and drained.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew before, spacing them a hand's width apart so the runners knit into a mat. Keep the soil evenly moist the first season and shear off spent flower spikes to tidy the planting. Short blue-violet flower spikes rise above the leaves in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, moist ground; forms slow non-running clumps.

directions: Plant in humus-rich soil that stays cool and moist, setting crowns level with the surface. This species stays as a compact rosette and does not spread by runners, so leave a little room between plants. Distinctive layered pyramids of blue flowers with leafy bracts open in spring.

planting.note: Sow shallowly in warm, gritty, alkaline soil after frost.

directions: Scatter seed on lean, chalky, well-drained soil and press it in lightly rather than burying it. This low, resin-scented plant tolerates drought and dislikes rich or soggy ground. Small yellow flowers dotted along the sprawling stems open through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a woodland spot with steady moisture.

directions: Site in a shaded, humus-rich bed and keep the soil consistently moist, as this clumping woodlander resents drying out. Set divisions at their former depth and mulch lightly. Toothed leaves carry unusually large blue-purple flowers in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; foliage colors best with some sun.

directions: Space divisions about a hand's width apart in moist, well-drained soil so the bronze-purple rosettes fill in quickly. Give a little sun to deepen the leaf color, and shear off old flower stalks after bloom. Blue flower spikes stand above the dark foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Set out in spring as a vigorous mat-forming groundcover.

directions: Plant divisions level with the soil surface and water them in well to establish the spreading runners. This fast, easygoing selection knits into a dense bronze-tinted carpet that smothers weeds. Spikes of blue-violet flowers rise a few inches above the leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade for the best tricolor foliage.

directions: Give this selection dappled light and evenly moist soil to bring out its rose, cream, and green variegation. Set divisions a hand's width apart and pull any all-green shoots that revert. Short blue flower spikes accent the colorful mat in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; give this large form a bit more elbow room.

directions: Space this vigorous selection wider than typical bugleweed to suit its oversized bronze-purple leaves. Plant divisions at their former depth in moist, fertile soil and keep them watered while establishing. Tall spikes of deep blue flowers, taller than most Ajuga, appear in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; more sun intensifies the near-black color.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained soil and give them some direct sun to deepen the glossy near-black, scalloped leaves. Avoid soggy winter ground, which this selection dislikes. Deep blue flower spikes contrast sharply with the dark foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; ideal as a fine-textured filler between stones.

directions: Tuck the small divisions closely together, since this miniature form has narrow chocolate-tinted leaves and stays very low. It threads nicely between pavers and tolerates light foot traffic. Slender blue flower spikes appear just above the tiny leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to protect the variegation.

directions: Set the small divisions close together in dappled light, where the cream, green, and burgundy tricolor leaves show best. Keep the soil evenly moist and remove any plain shoots that revert. Little blue flower spikes rise over the low variegated mat in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact cream-edged miniature for tight spots.

directions: Plant the small crowns closely in moist, well-drained soil with some shade to keep the cream leaf margins crisp. This tidy miniature spreads slowly and suits edging or trough plantings. Blue flower spikes stand just above the low foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; sun deepens the reddish-brown leaf tone.

directions: Set divisions a hand's width apart in well-drained soil and allow some sun to bring out the deep mahogany foliage. Shear off spent flower stalks to keep the mat neat. Blue-violet flower spikes appear above the dark leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade for reliable variegation.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained soil with dappled light to show off the bronze leaves splashed with pink and cream. Pull any reverted green shoots to preserve the coloring. Short spikes of blue flowers accent the mat in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a slower spreader than the green species.

directions: Give this cream-and-gray-green form part shade and steady moisture, spacing divisions a bit closer since it spreads slowly. Remove any all-green runners so the variegation stays true. Blue flower spikes appear over the leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; grown for its uncommon pink flower spikes.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained soil where the rose-pink flowers can be seen up close. Water well the first season to help the runners establish. Pink flower spikes rise above the green foliage in late spring instead of the usual blue.

planting.note: Plant in spring; brightens shady corners with white bloom.

directions: Set divisions a hand's width apart in cool, moist soil where the white flowers stand out against green leaves. Keep watered while the mat fills in and shear spent spikes afterward. Pure white flower spikes open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; some sun brings out the gold leaf edges.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained soil with morning sun to develop the green leaves rimmed in gold and cream. Protect from harsh afternoon heat, which can scorch the light margins. Short blue flower spikes appear over the bright mat in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade for the crisp white splashes.

directions: Set the compact divisions closely in dappled light so the narrow green leaves flecked with white knit together. Keep the soil evenly moist and remove reverted shoots. Blue flower spikes rise over the low speckled foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; grown for its tall pinkish-lilac spikes.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained soil where the taller flower spikes can be appreciated. Water in well and shear off the spent stalks after bloom. Upright pinkish-lilac flower spikes rise well above the green leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to keep the silvery cast.

directions: Give this form dappled light and even moisture to hold the frosted silvery-gray leaf color. Set divisions at their former depth and mulch lightly to conserve moisture. Blue flower spikes appear above the silvery mat in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; valued for softly gray-flushed foliage.

directions: Plant divisions in cool, moist, well-drained soil with some shade to bring out the gray-green tones. Keep watered while the mat fills and remove old flower stalks. Blue flower spikes rise above the muted foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a sturdy, upright-flowering selection.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained soil where the thick, glossy green leaves can form a robust mat. This selection stands up to weather better than many and needs little care. Dense spikes of blue flowers rise above the foliage in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; sun deepens the glossy chocolate-purple leaves.

directions: Set divisions a hand's width apart in well-drained soil and give them some sun to intensify the dark, shining bronze-purple foliage. Avoid wet winter soil to keep the mat healthy. Blue flower spikes contrast with the dark leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a dwarf pink-flowered form for small spaces.

directions: Plant the compact divisions closely in moist, well-drained soil where the dwarf habit suits edging or containers. Keep watered while establishing and shear off spent spikes. Short rose-pink flower spikes appear above the small leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; part of the vigorous Feathered Friends series.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained soil where this heat-tolerant, robust selection can spread into a full mat. Its larger leaves show green and deep purple tones through the season. Tall blue flower spikes rise above the foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; grown for chartreuse-to-gold new foliage.

directions: Give this Feathered Friends selection part shade and steady moisture so the bright chartreuse and gold leaves color well without scorching. Set divisions at their former depth and water in. Blue flower spikes contrast with the golden foliage in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; multicolored foliage in the Feathered Friends line.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained soil with dappled light to display the shifting purple, rose, and green leaf tones. This vigorous selection knits into a dense, weather-tolerant mat. Blue flower spikes rise above the colorful leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a slow, clumping novelty rather than a runner.

directions: Plant in cool, moist, well-drained soil, spacing crowns closely since this slow clumper does not send out runners. Its crinkled, metallic bronze leaves have an almost puckered texture. Deep blue flower spikes rise from the tight rosettes in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a moist woodland bed; clumps rather than runs.

directions: Site in a shaded, humus-rich spot with steady moisture, since this clumping woodlander dislikes drying out. Set divisions at their former depth and mulch to keep roots cool. Large, showy blue flowers appear above the toothed leaves in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the soil can be worked.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew before and firm the soil around the crown. This vigorous selection carries an abundance of chartreuse flower sprays over mounded, softly hairy leaves. Cut plants back hard after the first flush to encourage a tidy rebound and possible rebloom.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring for the best establishment.

directions: Set divisions into humus-rich soil and water them in well during the first weeks. This upright-flowering form holds its lime-green sprays cleanly above compact foliage. Shear off the fading blooms to keep the clump neat and limit unwanted seedlings.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring while growth is fresh.

directions: Give this larger, more robust selection room to spread and set it in moist, well-drained ground. The bold clumps produce billowing masses of yellow-green flowers in early summer. Trim the whole plant back after flowering to refresh the mound of foliage.

planting.note: Set out young plants near the last frost in a sunny, gritty spot.

directions: Surface-sow seed and give it a cold period to break dormancy before warmth. This compact alpine species has small, deeply lobed leaves edged with silvery hairs beneath, ideal for rock gardens and edging. Tiny greenish-yellow flowers appear in loose clusters through summer above the silver-margined foliage.

planting.note: Plant out near the last frost in a spot with good drainage.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it cool and moist for germination. This dwarf species forms tidy blue-green mounds and develops reddish stems as the season advances. Sprays of chartreuse flowers hover over the small pleated leaves in early summer.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings out around the last spring frost.

directions: Surface-sow and chill the seed briefly to improve sprouting rates. This meadow species of Europe forms rounded, softly hairy leaves that hold dew in glistening beads. Loose clusters of tiny yellow-green flowers open across summer.

planting.note: Set plants out near the last frost in moist, cool ground.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep it consistently moist and cool. This compact species stays lower and tidier than common lady's mantle, with glossy, shallowly lobed leaves. Airy chartreuse flower heads appear in early to midsummer and are excellent for the front of a border.

planting.note: Plant out near the last frost in a gritty, well-drained bed.

directions: Surface-sow and give the seed a cool spell before warmth to prompt germination. Star-shaped leaves are divided into narrow lobes with silvery, silky-haired undersides that catch the light. Small greenish-yellow flowers rise on slender stems through summer, suiting rock gardens and edging.

planting.note: Plant in spring and give it room, since the airy clump widens each year.

directions: Sow seed after a cold moist chill or set out divisions in spring, spacing generously to show off the mounded shape. Full sun and lean, well-drained soil keep the fine needle-like leaves dense and self-supporting. Soft blue flowers open in late spring, but the real show is the brilliant gold fall foliage that earned it Perennial Plant of the Year honors.

planting.note: Best planted in spring in a spot with room to form a large clump.

directions: Give seed a cold stratification period before sowing, as this streamside native germinates slowly. It tolerates heavier, moister soils than most bluestars and grows tall and shrubby in full sun. Glossy willowy leaves back light blue spring flowers, and the whole plant gilds to yellow before frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sharply drained, sandy ground.

directions: Sow after cold chilling and expect uneven germination from this sandhill native. It thrives in dry, sandy soil and full sun where richer conditions would make it lanky. Fine, almost hair-like leaves give it a soft texture, and pale blue stars appear in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a hot, well-drained bed.

directions: This threadleaf variety wants the same cold-moist seed treatment and quick-draining soil as the species. Plant in full sun where its extremely narrow leaves create a fine, feathery clump. Enjoy pale blue spring flowers followed by good golden fall color on drought-tough plants.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, fast-draining soil in a rock garden or dry border.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and grow this compact western species in sharply drained, mineral soil. It stays low and stout, resenting rich beds and standing water, so treat it like a xeric rock-garden plant. Thick blue-green leaves set off clusters of soft blue flowers in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this is the compact European-Asian bluestar once called Rhazya orientalis.

directions: Chill seed before sowing or divide established clumps in early spring. This tidy, slow-spreading species suits smaller gardens and holds its shape without staking in full sun. Deeper violet-blue flowers cluster at the stem tips over a long stretch of late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist to average soil in full sun.

directions: Give seed the usual cold-moist chill and grow this southeastern native in sun with steady moisture. Rigid upright stems carry narrow leaves and hold themselves well without support. Pale blue stars open in late spring above foliage that colors nicely in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm bed with good drainage.

directions: Cold-stratify seed before sowing this Gulf Coast species. The undersides of its leaves are softly hairy and silvery, a distinctive touch in the border. Soft blue flowers appear in spring on drought-tolerant plants once they are established.

planting.note: Plant in spring in average, well-drained garden soil.

directions: Chill seed before sowing this east Asian species, which resembles the eastern bluestar but with broader leaves. Grow in full sun to light shade in ordinary soil that drains freely. Clusters of pale blue flowers open in late spring above clean green foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, fast-draining soil in a warm site.

directions: Sow chilled seed and grow this southwestern desert species in sharp-draining, mineral soil with little supplemental water. It stays compact and appreciates heat and full exposure. As the name suggests, its blue flowers are notably larger than most bluestars, opening in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, dry, sharply drained spot.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and treat this southwestern species as a xeric plant in gritty, arid soil. It stays small and drought-hardy, disliking rich beds and overwatering. Its flowers have unusually long, slender tubes and open pale blue in late spring and summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in very fast-draining desert soil.

directions: Give seed a cold-moist period, then grow this Mojave native in gravelly, arid soil with full sun and minimal irrigation. Some forms are softly hairy, giving the plant a gray-green cast. Clusters of small blue-white flowers appear in spring on this tough, low desert plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a dry, gravelly bed in full sun.

directions: Sow chilled seed and grow this Arizona endemic in lean, sharply drained soil with plenty of heat. It is naturally drought-adapted and needs little water once rooted in. Pale blue star flowers open in spring on narrow-leaved stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Cold-stratify seed before sowing this southwestern species suited to hot, dry gardens. Grow it in gritty soil with sharp drainage and full sun for the healthiest plants. Small pale blue flowers appear in spring on this heat-loving native.

planting.note: Plant in spring in average, well-drained soil in sun to light shade.

directions: This narrow-leaved variety of eastern bluestar takes the same cold-moist seed treatment as the species. Its slender willow-like leaves give a finer, more graceful texture in the border. Powder-blue flowers open in late spring, and the foliage turns golden in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this naturally shorter form suits the front of a border.

directions: This compact mountain variety of eastern bluestar is grown just like the species but stays lower and denser. Cold-stratify seed or divide clumps in spring, and place it toward the front of a sunny bed. It blooms early and heavily with clusters of soft blue stars, then turns yellow in autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted plants in spring at the front of a border.

directions: Set this compact selection in full sun and average soil, spacing plants to let the low mounds knit together. Because it does not come true from seed, propagate by dividing clumps in spring. It is prized for unusually deep periwinkle-blue flowers that open in late spring on a tidy, short plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the dark emerging stems can be admired up close.

directions: Grow this selection in full sun and divide it in spring to keep it true, as seedlings will not match the parent. Its new spring stems emerge nearly black, contrasting with silver-veined leaves and light blue flowers. The dark stems fade to green as the season goes on, and the foliage turns gold in fall.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring in full sun for the sturdiest habit.

directions: This compact, well-branched selection is grown in full sun and propagated vegetatively rather than from seed. It forms a dense, rounded clump of glossy dark green leaves that resists flopping. Clusters of blue star flowers appear in late spring, and the foliage turns golden yellow in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this dwarf threadleaf form fits smaller gardens.

directions: Grow this compact threadleaf selection in full sun and divide it to keep it uniform, since it will not come true from seed. It offers the same fine, needle-like texture as Arkansas bluestar but on a shorter, denser mound. Soft blue flowers open in late spring, followed by the brilliant gold fall color the species is known for.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun where it has room to reach full size.

directions: This vigorous selection is grown in full sun and divided in spring to keep it true to type. It makes a tall, shrubby clump with narrow leaves and holds up well without staking. Deeper sky-blue flowers cluster at the stem tips in late spring above healthy green foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in a well-drained bed.

directions: This hybrid selection bridges the broad-leaved and threadleaf bluestars, with intermediate foliage that is finer than eastern bluestar but not as needle-thin as the Arkansas type. Grow it in full sun and propagate by division to preserve the cross. Blue star flowers open in late spring, and the leaves color to warm yellow in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sharply drained, sandy soil as a low groundcover.

directions: This unusually flat, ground-hugging selection of fringed bluestar spreads wide while staying only a few inches tall. Grow it in full sun and lean, sandy soil, and propagate by division to keep the prostrate habit. Pale blue flowers dot the mat in spring, and the fine foliage turns yellow before frost.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring once the soil has warmed and stays evenly moist.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil surface in rich, moisture-retentive ground and water deeply through the first summer. Give it room to spread by underground runners and avoid disturbing established clumps, as they resent transplanting. Look for the tall wands of single white flowers with golden centers from late summer well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring into humus-rich soil in light shade or morning sun.

directions: Site divisions where the soil stays cool and moist and keep the crown at ground level. Stake taller stems in windy gardens and cut spent stalks back after bloom. Semi-double white blooms with twisted petals open from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile soil that does not dry out in summer.

directions: Set the crown flush with the soil and mulch to conserve moisture through hot spells. Allow the clump to settle in undisturbed for a couple of seasons before it hits full stride. Silvery rose-pink single flowers rise on wiry stems from late summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained soil with afternoon shade in hot regions.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and water regularly the first year to establish deep roots. Deadhead to keep the display tidy and let it naturalize gently over time. Large semi-double pink flowers appear from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, retentive soil in light shade.

directions: Keep the crown at ground level and provide steady moisture, especially in the establishment year. Divide crowded clumps in early spring only when vigor declines. Deep rose-pink semi-double flowers with narrow petals bloom from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in dappled light.

directions: Set the crown flush with the soil surface and mulch lightly to hold moisture. Leave clumps undisturbed to reach their full flowering potential. Deep rosy-pink flowers with uneven petal shading open from late summer through autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the hardiest and most vigorous of the fall anemones.

directions: Give this spreader plenty of space, as it colonizes readily by running roots. Plant the crown at soil level and water through dry summers to keep the grape-shaped foliage lush. Soft silvery-pink single flowers appear early and continue into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich soil in part shade for the best color.

directions: Plant with the crown at ground level and keep soil evenly moist during flowering. This more compact selection suits smaller borders and containers. Rich rose double flowers with a cupped form bloom from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into humus-rich, moist soil.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and water deeply until established. Allow it to knit into an undisturbed clump over a few seasons. Warm pink semi-double flowers appear on branching stems from late summer through autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in light shade.

directions: Keep the crown at ground level and mulch to hold summer moisture. Cut stems back after flowering and leave clumps to establish undisturbed. Clean white single flowers with a boss of yellow stamens bloom from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich soil that stays evenly moist.

directions: Set divisions with the crown flush to the soil and water regularly the first summer. Give it space to form a substantial clump over time. Large soft-pink single flowers open on tall stems from late summer through autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade with steady summer moisture.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level and keep the ground cool and moist. This compact selection works at the front of a shaded border. Rosy-red semi-double flowers appear from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, retentive soil in dappled light.

directions: Set the crown flush with the soil surface and water through dry weather. Leave established clumps in place to reach full size. Vivid magenta-pink single flowers rise on slender stems from late summer through autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, well-drained soil in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant at soil level and keep evenly moist, deadheading spent blooms to prolong the show. This long-flowering hybrid produces waves of bloom from late spring to fall rather than a single autumn flush. White cupped flowers with a distinctive blue-violet reverse open and close with the light.

planting.note: Plant in spring in retentive soil in sun to light shade.

directions: Set the crown at ground level and water consistently for repeat flowering through the season. Remove faded stems to encourage fresh buds. Semi-double white flowers backed with lavender-blue streaking bloom from early summer into autumn.
